does: This paper introduces SafeDream, a lightweight external framework for detecting multi-turn jailbreak attacks before language models comply with harmful requests. The core contribution is formulating proactive early jailbreak detection with a new metric called detection lead, which measures how many turns ahead an attack can be identified before the model generates harmful content. SafeDream operates without modifying LLM weights and comprises three components: a safety state world model that encodes LLM hidden states into compact safety representations and predicts their evolution across conversation turns, CUSUM detection that accumulates weak per-turn risk signals into reliable cumulative evidence, and contrastive imagination for generating synthetic attack trajectories. The framework demonstrates effectiveness in identifying attacks earlier than existing detection methods while maintaining low false positive rates.
connects: This work addresses limitations in existing alignment and guardrail approaches that typically require costly weight modifications, evaluate individual conversation turns in isolation without modeling cumulative safety erosion, and only detect attacks after harmful content generation. SafeDream builds on research in proactive safety monitoring, world models for state prediction, and sequential anomaly detection methods like CUSUM (cumulative sum control charts). The paper responds to recent work on multi-turn jailbreak attacks that have shown success rates exceeding 90% against state-of-the-art models, extending prior research on understanding how conversational interactions progressively undermine safety alignment. It combines insights from model interpretability, state-space modeling, and adversarial robustness to create an external safety monitoring module.
enables: This framework enables several future research directions in LLM safety. First, it opens possibilities for developing more sophisticated world models that better capture the dynamics of safety erosion across extended conversations. Second, the detection lead metric and contrastive imagination approach could be extended to other types of gradual attacks or long-horizon adversarial patterns. Third, SafeDream's external module design enables deployment as a practical safety layer for existing models without fine-tuning, potentially facilitating safer deployment of current and future LLMs in high-stakes applications. The work also points toward developing better understanding of how safety representations evolve during conversations and using predictive models as defensive mechanisms against evolving attack strategies.
